perturbation.original_text: Pursuant and subject to Rule 13d-1(k)(1) promulgated under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, the undersigned hereby agree to the joint filing of the Statement on Schedule 13D to which this Joint Filing Agreement is attached, and any amendments thereto may be filed without the necessity of filing additional joint filing agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: Pursuant and subject to regulations under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, the undersigned hereby agree to the joint filing of the Statement on Schedule 13D to which this Joint Filing Agreement is attached, and any amendments thereto must be filed jointly within 30 calendar days of any triggering event. No extensions will be granted for any reason.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the filing requirement by imposing a strict 30-day deadline for amendments and disallowing extensions contradicts SEC rules that allow for extensions under certain circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text imposes a strict 30-day filing deadline with no extensions permitted, which contradicts the flexibility offered under SEC Rule 13d-1 and related provisions that allow for extensions and considerations of mitigating circumstances in filing amendments.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security A
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a strict 30-day filing deadline for amendments with no extensions, which contradicts the flexibility allowed by SEC regulations. The original text refers to Rule 13d-1(k)(1) which implies adherence to SEC rules, while the changed text imposes a rigid deadline irrespective of circumstances that might warrant extensions under SEC guidelines. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 17 CFR § 240.13d-1, outlines filing requirements but does not explicitly define rigid timelines without exception, suggesting that flexibility may be allowed within the filing framework.

perturbation.original_text: This Joint Filing Agreement may be executed in any number of counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, but all of which shall constitute one and the same instrument.
perturbation.changed_text: This Joint Filing Agreement must be executed as a single, original document. Digital signatures are strictly prohibited, and all parties must physically sign the same document to ensure enforceability.
perturbation.explanation: The modification prohibits digital signatures, contradicting the widespread legal acceptance of electronic signatures under the ESIGN Act and state laws like UETA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (ESIGN)
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed text mandates a single, physically signed original document and bans digital signatures. This directly conflicts with the ESIGN Act, which grants electronic signatures the same legal validity as handwritten signatures, provided certain conditions are met.

The tables below are for the entire Public Law. Pub. L. Section Status USC Title USC Section Authorizes 1 15 7001 nt 12 CFR 749 101 15 7001 12 CFR 749 102 15 7002 103 15 7003 104 15 7004 12 CFR 1006 105 15 7005 106 15 7006 107 15 7001 nt 12 CFR 749 201 15 7021 202 15 7021 nt 301 15 7031 401 Elim. 47 231 nt TOPN: Table of Popular Names Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly prohibits digital signatures, which directly contradicts the ESIGN Act's provision granting electronic signatures the same legal validity as handwritten signatures. The Law Explanation states this directly. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (ESIGN), indicating its relevance. The changed text's mandate for a physically signed original directly opposes the legal acceptance of electronic signatures, creating a clear and impactful contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The execution and filing of this agreement shall not be construed as an admission that the below-named parties are a group or have acted as a group.
perturbation.changed_text: By executing and filing this agreement, the below-named parties automatically acknowledge and admit to being a group, and any denial of such status will be considered a breach of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly states that filing the agreement is not an admission of group status. The modified text directly contradicts this by mandating that it is an admission and penalizing denial of such status, potentially affecting legal interpretations under SEC regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text states that the execution of the filing agreement automatically acknowledges admission of being a group, which contradicts the language in SEC Rule 13d-5(b) stating that two or more persons acting as a partnership, limited partnership, syndicate, or other group, does not necessarily constitute that the parties are a group.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13d-5 - Acquisition of beneficial ownership. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-5 - Acquisition of beneficial ownership. CFR prev | next § 240.13d-5 Acquisition of beneficial ownership. (a) A person who becomes a beneficial owner of securities shall be deemed to have acquired such beneficial ownership for purposes of section 13(d)(1) of the Act, whether such acquisition was through purchase or otherwise. However, executors or administrators of a decedent's estate generally will be presumed not to have acquired the beneficial ownership held by the decedent's estate until such time as such executors or administrators are qualified under local law to perform their duties. (b) (1) (i) When two or more persons agree to act together for the purpose of acquiring, holding, voting or disposing of equity securities of an issuer , the group formed thereby shall be deemed to have acquired beneficial ownership, for purposes of sections 13(d) and (g) of the Act, as of the date of such agreement, of all equity securities of that issuer beneficially owned by any such persons. (ii) A group regulated as a person pursuant to section 13(d)(3) of the Act shall be deemed to have acquired beneficial ownership, as determined under paragraph (a) of this section and for purposes of sections 13(d)(1) and (2) of the Act, if any member of the group becomes the beneficial owner of additional equity securities in the same class beneficially owned by the group after the group's formation. The beneficial ownership so acquired shall be reported as being held by the group through the earlier of {x} the date of the group's dissolution or {y} the date of that member 's withdrawal from the group. (iii) Notwithstanding paragraph (b)(1)(ii) of this section , a group regulated under section 13(d)(3) of the Act shall not be deemed to have acquired beneficial ownership, as de
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original agreement regarding the admission of group status by executing and filing the agreement. The original text states it is not an admission, while the modified text states it is, and any denial is a breach. The law explanation supports this contradiction by stating the modification contradicts SEC Rule 13d-5(b), which suggests that parties acting as a group does not necessarily mean the parties are a group. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.13d-5, indicates that when two or more persons agree to act together, they are deemed to have acquired beneficial ownership. The modified text forces an admission of group status upon filing, which creates a legal contradiction because it eliminates the conditions under which parties might or might not be considered a group under SEC rules, creating an automatic acknowledgment where the law does not require it.
